mnemonics: BROOKS COLE: B-R-O-O-K-S stands for Best Results Often Obtained through Knowledge of Systems. This reminds you that optimization often involves finding the best (optimal) results by understanding and working with entire systems (matrices, vector spaces) rather than just individual equations.

DUALITY: Don't Underestimate A Linear's Yielding Truth. This helps remember that every primal linear programming problem has a dual, and understanding their relationship is key to solving optimization problems.
keyPoints: Optimization in linear algebra involves finding maximum or minimum values of a linear function subject to linear constraints.
The Simplex Method is a fundamental algorithm for solving linear programming problems by moving from one vertex of the feasible region to another.
Duality Theory states that every linear programming problem (the primal) has a related dual problem, and their solutions are intimately connected.
The Fundamental Theorem of Linear Programming guarantees that if an optimal solution exists, it will occur at a vertex of the feasible region.
Sensitivity Analysis examines how changes in coefficients or constraints affect the optimal solution.
commonMistakes: Forgetting to check for feasibility before applying the simplex method. An infeasible problem has no solution.
Misinterpreting slack and surplus variables when converting inequalities to standard form for the simplex tableau.
Confusing the primal and dual problems, leading to incorrect formulation and solution.
Failing to recognize when a problem is unbounded (the objective function can improve indefinitely) versus when it has an optimal solution.
Assuming all linear programming problems have a unique solution; some have multiple optimal solutions or none at all.
